name: Daikoku
description: Daikoku, often referred to as Daikokuten, is one of the Seven Lucky Gods (Shichi Fukujin) in Japanese folklore. He is syncretically associated with both the Shinto deity Ōkuninushi and the Buddhist deity Mahākāla. Daikoku is primarily revered as the god of wealth, prosperity, commerce, and agriculture. He is typically depicted as a stout, smiling man wearing a hat and holding a golden mallet, which he uses to produce wealth when struck. He is often shown standing on bales of rice, symbolizing abundance and a bountiful harvest. Daikoku's benevolent nature extends to protecting farmers from evil spirits and ensuring plentiful yields. His association with the kitchen as a provider of food and sustenance further solidifies his role as a vital deity in Japanese households. His popularity grew during the Edo period, solidifying his place in Japanese culture as a bringer of good fortune and prosperity.
subtitle: God of Wealth and Prosperity

keyMyths.title: Daikoku and the Rat
keyMyths.description: According to legend, Daikoku is often accompanied by a rat, which is considered his messenger. The rat is believed to bring him messages and guide him to sources of wealth and prosperity. The rat's association with Daikoku stems from its natural ability to find food and resources, symbolizing Daikoku's own capacity to bring abundance. The rat is also revered as a symbol of fertility and resourcefulness, further emphasizing Daikoku's role as a provider of sustenance and prosperity. Some believe the rat protects Daikoku's rice bales from other vermin.
keyMyths.source: Hearn, Lafcadio. *Gleanings in Buddha-Fields: Studies of Hand and Soul in the Far East.* Houghton, Mifflin and Company, 1897.

keyMyths.title: Daikoku and Ōkuninushi
keyMyths.description: Daikoku is syncretically associated with the Shinto deity Ōkuninushi, the god of nation-building, agriculture, and medicine. This association likely arose due to similarities in their roles as providers and protectors of the people. In some regions, Daikoku is considered a manifestation of Ōkuninushi, further blurring the lines between Shinto and Buddhist traditions. This fusion highlights the adaptability and syncretism inherent in Japanese religious beliefs, where different deities and traditions often merge and coexist harmoniously.
keyMyths.source: Kitagawa, Joseph Mitsuo. *Religion in Japanese History.* Columbia University Press, 1966.

keyMyths.title: The Golden Mallet
keyMyths.description: Daikoku's most iconic symbol is his golden mallet (Uchide no Kozuchi). When struck, this magical mallet is said to produce anything the wielder desires, most commonly wealth and prosperity. This symbolizes Daikoku's power to bring abundance and good fortune to those who invoke his favor. The mallet's association with wish-fulfillment further emphasizes Daikoku's role as a benevolent deity who grants blessings and helps people achieve their desires. It is a potent symbol of wish fulfillment and prosperity.
keyMyths.source: Roberts, Jeremy. *Japanese Mythology A to Z.* Chelsea House Publishers, 2010.

sources.author: Addiss, Stephen
